title: Topological protection of local quantum Fisher information
abstract: In many-body quantum systems, unitary dynamics generically delocalize locally encoded information, causing single-site metrological sensitivity to vanish. We analytically demonstrate that a topological phase can prevent this dispersal. In the open Kitaev chain, a Majorana zero mode fixes the boundary quantum Fisher information (QFI) at a nonzero plateau that persists for times exponentially long in system size. We derive exact analytical expressions for the local QFI and identify the mechanism as the spatial separation of the two Majorana quadratures to opposite ends of the chain. This separation produces a boundary encoding-axis asymmetry that distinguishes topological boundary memory from a generic localized subgap signal. We show numerically that the asymmetry is robust to moderate quenched on-site disorder, while the boundary plateau remains visible under parity-preserving interactions in finite-size real-time simulations. The protocol requires only product-state initialization, Hamiltonian evolution, and single-site readout.
summary: The paper demonstrates that topological phases can protect local quantum information from the scrambling effects of many-body dynamics. By utilizing Majorana zero modes, the authors show that a nonzero boundary Quantum Fisher Information can be maintained for exponentially long times. This effect is characterized by a distinct asymmetry in encoding channels that is robust to both disorder and interactions.
main_problem: In many-body systems, unitary dynamics typically cause locally encoded information to delocalize and vanish. The authors seek to demonstrate how topological phases can prevent this dispersal to provide a form of topological metrological memory.
main_result: The authors show that in the topological phase of a Kitaev chain, a Majorana zero mode pins the boundary Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) to a nonzero plateau that persists for exponentially long times. They identify a unique boundary encoding-axis asymmetry as a robust diagnostic for this topological protection.
method: The study uses an analytical approach via Bogoliubov-de Gennes formalism and Jordan-Wigner transformation, complemented by real-time numerical simulations of finite-size chains to test robustness against disorder and interactions.
model_or_system: An anisotropic XY spin-1/2 chain with open boundaries, which maps to a 1D Kitaev chain (topological superconductor) under the Jordan-Wigner transformation.
key_observables: Local Quantum Fisher Information (QFI), boundary-axis asymmetry (comparing R_y and R_x encoding channels), and the Majorana localization length.
important_parameters: Transverse field (h), anisotropy (gamma), system size (L), Majorana localization length (xi), and the energy scales of the bulk gap and Majorana splitting.
main_assumptions: The protocol assumes a specific time-averaging window to isolate stationary signals from dephasing; the readout is assumed to be local at the boundary to avoid the non-locality of the Jordan-Wigner string.
figures_summary: The notes do not provide specific figure descriptions, though they mention the presence of figures illustrating the boundary QFI plateau and the asymmetry between encoding channels.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of information delocalization, defines the XY/Kitaev model, provides analytical derivations for the QFI and its topological plateau, presents numerical evidence for robustness against disorder and interactions, and concludes with scaling analysis near the phase transition.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and quantum information as it proposes a way to use topological protection to preserve metrological sensitivity, offering a robust protocol for local information encoding and readout in noisy environments.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Time-averaged boundary QFI F (1) Q across the (h/J, γ ≥0) phase diagram (boundary encoding and readout, k=j=1, averaging window tJ ∈[150, 200]); dashed line: |h|=J. (b) Topological phase (γ=0.3, h=0.5J): ballistic spreading coexists with a persistent boundary streak from the Majorana zero mode. (c) Trivial phase (γ=0.8, h=1.8J): complete dephasing with no persistent boundary signal. Here: L=100, k=1, θ0=0.

FIG. 2. Mechanism of the boundary metrological plateau (L=100, γ=1, h=0.1J, j=1, averaging window tJ ∈[150, 200]). (a) Encoding-site scan: the time-averaged boundary QFI de- cays as ∝e−2(k−1)/ξ, directly resolving the Majorana local- ization length. (b) Boundary encoding-axis asymmetry: for left-boundary readout and γ > 0, the ˆRy channel sustains the plateau, while the orthogonal ˆRx channel is strongly sup- pressed and vanishes in the thermodynamic limit.

FIG. 4. Robustness of the boundary metrological plateau. (a) Disorder: disorder-averaged boundary QFI versus disorder strength W/J (h=0.5J, γ=1, L=200, Ndis=1000). The ˆRy channel (pink) preserves the plateau for W ≲εgap while ˆRx (purple) remains suppressed; shaded bands show one standard deviation. For disorder strengths above the clean bulk-gap scale, both channels converge. (b) Interactions: time-averaged boundary QFI versus interaction strength δ/J for γ=1 (blue), γ=0.5 (red), at h=0 (dark) and h=0.2J (light). System sizes L=12 (dotted), 16 (dashed), 20 (solid) show weak size de- pendence. Parameters: tJ ∈[25, 50] averaging window, ˆRy encoding, k=j=1.
FIG. 3. Critical scaling of the boundary QFI near the topologi- cal transition. For fixed γ=1 and boundary encoding/readout, the data for L ∈{50, 100, 200, 400} approach the asymptotic
